questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
This question is about salts.
Green copper carbonate and sulfuric acid can be used to produce blue copper sulfate crystals.
Excess copper carbonate is added to sulfuric acid.
Give three observations you would make.
1
2
3
questions.mark_scheme:
any three from:
• green solid / powder
• colourless solution
• blue solution formed
• copper carbonate disappears
• fizzing / effervescence or bubbles (of gas)
• stops fizzing
• solid / powder left at the end or copper carbonate left at the end
questions.extra_notes:
ignore green copper carbonate
allow colour (of solution) changes
allow solid disappears
ignore gas
allow fizzing slows down
allow (container) gets hot
or
allow temperature increases

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text: How can the excess copper carbonate be removed?
questions.mark_scheme: filtration or filter

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
The pH of the solution changes during the reaction.
What is the pH of the solution at the end of the reaction?
pH = ____

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Copper carbonate and sulfuric acid react to produce copper sulfate.
What type of reaction is this?
questions.mark_scheme: neutralisation
questions.extra_notes: allow exothermic

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Ammonium nitrate is a salt.
Figure 1 shows the maximum mass of ammonium nitrate that can dissolve in 100 cm3 of water at different temperatures.
Figure 1
A student adds ammonium nitrate to water at 80 °C until no more dissolves.
The student cools 100 cm3 of this solution of ammonium nitrate from 80 °C to 20 °C to produce crystals of ammonium nitrate.
Determine the mass of ammonium nitrate that crystallises on cooling 100 cm3 of this solution from 80 °C to 20 °C
Mass = ____ g
questions.extra_notes:
allow a value in range 82–84 (g at 80 °C)
allow a value in range 32–33 (g at 20 °C)
allow a correct calculation using incorrectly read values for mass at 80 °C and/or 20 °C

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
This question is about electrolysis.
Figure 2 shows the apparatus used to investigate the electrolysis of potassium sulfate solution.
Figure 2
Potassium sulfate contains K+ and SO4 2– ions.
What is the formula of potassium sulfate?
Tick () one box.

|   |   |
|---|---|
|   | KSO4 |
|   | K2SO4 |
|   | K(SO4)2 |
|   | K2(SO4)2 |
questions.mark_scheme: K2SO4

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
What are the volumes of gases collected in the electrolysis experiment?
Use Figure 2.
Volume of hydrogen = ____ cm3
Volume of oxygen = ____ cm3
questions.mark_scheme:
(volume of hydrogen) 30 (cm³)
and
(volume of oxygen) 15 (cm³)

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
A student made the following hypothesis:
‘The volumes of gases collected in this electrolysis experiment are in the same ratio as hydrogen atoms to oxygen atoms in a water molecule.’
Explain how the volumes of gases collected in the experiment in Figure 2 support the student’s hypothesis.
Use your answer to Question 02.2
questions.mark_scheme:
(because) the ratio of volume of hydrogen : oxygen is 2 : 1
(and this is the) same as the ratio of hydrogen (atoms) : oxygen (atoms) in (formula of) H2O

OR

(because) the ratio of volume of hydrogen : oxygen is not 2 : 1
(and this is) different to the ratio of hydrogen (atoms) : oxygen (atoms) in (formula of) H2O
questions.extra_notes: must relate to the volumes given in question 02.2

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
The experiment is repeated 4 times.
The volumes of oxygen collected in the 4 experiments are:
6 cm3 9 cm3 10 cm3 11 cm3
The mean volume of oxygen collected in the 4 experiments is 9 cm3
The measure of uncertainty is the range of a set of measurements about the mean.
What is the measure of uncertainty in the 4 experiments?
Tick () one box.

|   |   |
|---|---|
|   | 9 ± 1 cm3 |
|   | 9 ± 2 cm3 |
|   | 9 ± 3 cm3 |
questions.mark_scheme: 9 ±3 cm³

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
The potassium sulfate solution has 0.86 g of potassium sulfate dissolved in 25 cm3 of water.
Calculate the mass of potassium sulfate needed to make 1.0 dm3 of solution.
Mass = ____ g
questions.mark_scheme:
(conversion)
25/1000 = 0.025 (dm³)

(concentration =) 0.86/0.025 = 34.4 (g per dm³)

OR

(conversion)
1000/25 = 40
(40 × 0.86) = 34.4 (g per dm³)

OR

(concentration =) 0.86/25 = 0.0344
(conversion) (0.0344 × 1000) = 34.4 (g per dm³)
questions.extra_notes:
allow correct use of incorrect / no conversion 
allow 34 (g per dm³)
allow correct use of incorrect / no conversion 
allow 34 (g per dm³)
allow 34 (g per dm³)

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
This question is about Group 7 elements.
What are the Group 7 elements known as?
questions.mark_scheme: halogens

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text: Why do Group 7 elements react in similar ways?
questions.mark_scheme: all have 7 electrons in outer shell OR all have 7 outer electrons
questions.extra_notes:
allow energy level for shell
allow same number of outer electrons
allow one electron required to complete the outer shell

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Figure 3 shows the structure of a molecule of chlorine oxide.
Figure 3
What is the molecular formula of the chlorine oxide molecule in Figure 3?
questions.mark_scheme: Cl2O7

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Figure 4 shows the melting points of some Group 7 elements.
Figure 4
The melting point of fluorine is –220 °C
Complete Figure 4.
You should:
• complete the scale on the y-axis
• draw the bar for the melting point of fluorine.
questions.mark_scheme:
y-axis scale correct from –100 to –250 °C
bar correctly plotted at –220 °C
questions.extra_notes: allow a tolerance of ±½ a small square

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Explain the trend in the melting points of the Group 7 elements. 
Use Figure 4.
questions.mark_scheme:
(the) molecules increase in size going down the group
(so the) forces between the molecules increase OR (so the) intermolecular forces increase
(so the) melting points increase going down the group OR (so the) melting points increase with increasing relative atomic mass
questions.extra_notes:
allow converse explanation in terms of decreasing melting point
allow atoms increase in size going down the group
allow increase in number of electron shells going down group
allow (so) more energy is needed to separate the molecules

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
What is the state symbol for bromine at –50 °C?
Use Figure 4.
Tick () one box.

|   |   |
|---|---|
|   | (aq) |
|   | (g) |
|   | (l) |
|   | (s) |

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Evaporation and boiling occur at the surface of bromine at its boiling point.
Name one more process that happens at the surface of bromine at its boiling point.
questions.mark_scheme: condensation
questions.extra_notes:
allow condensing
ignore evaporating and boiling

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
This question is about structure and bonding.
Figure 5 represents part of a carbon molecule.
Figure 5
Name the type of carbon molecule in Figure 5.
questions.mark_scheme: fullerene
questions.extra_notes:
allow (carbon) nanotube
do not accept 
Buckministerfullerene

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text: Suggest one property that makes the carbon molecule in Figure 5 useful in nanotechnology.
questions.mark_scheme:
any one from:
• conducts heat
• conducts electricity
• very high length to diameter ratio
questions.extra_notes:
allow large surface area to volume ratio
allow high tensile strength
allow can trap other molecules / atoms / ions

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
An alloy of aluminium contains small amounts of other metals.
Explain why other metals are added to aluminium.
questions.mark_scheme:
other metal atoms have different sizes to aluminium atoms
(so) the layers of aluminium atoms are distorted
(so) the layers cannot slide
(which) makes the alloy harder
questions.extra_notes:
allow (so) the atoms cannot slide over each other
allow (which) makes the alloy stronger

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Figure 6 represents part of the structure of a polymer.
Figure 6
Compare the bonding within the chains with the forces between the chains in this polymer.
questions.mark_scheme:
covalent bonds (between atoms) in the chain
intermolecular forces between the chains
covalent bonds are strong and intermolecular forces are weak

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
This question is about hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid.
Complete the dot and cross diagram to represent the bonding in hydrogen chloride on Figure 7.
Use dots (o) and crosses (x) to represent electrons.
You should show only the electrons in the outer shells.
Figure 7
questions.mark_scheme:
one shared pair in overlap
6 non-bonding electrons in outer shell of chlorine
questions.extra_notes:
allow any combination of circles, dots, crosses or e(–)
do not accept extra electron(s) on outer shell of hydrogen
ignore any inner shell electrons
an answer of
scores 2 marks

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Hydrogen chloride dissolves in water to produce hydrochloric acid.
Hydrochloric acid is a strong acid.
What is meant by the term strong acid?
questions.mark_scheme: completely ionises in aqueous solution
questions.extra_notes: allow completely dissociates in aqueous solution

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text: Describe how magnesium can be used to distinguish between a strong acid and a weak acid of the same concentration.
questions.mark_scheme: fizzing / effervescence or magnesium disappears at a greater rate with a strong acid
questions.extra_notes:
allow converse with weak acid
allow for 2 marks strong acid has a greater temperature increase

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
The concentration of hydrochloric acid is increased by a factor of 100
What is the change in pH?
questions.mark_scheme: (pH) decreases by (a unit of) 2
questions.extra_notes: allow pH is lower

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Ethene and hydrogen chloride react to produce chloroethane.
The displayed formulae equation for the reaction is:
The reaction is exothermic.
In the reaction the energy released forming new bonds is 56 kJ/mol greater than the energy needed to break existing bonds.
Table 1 shows some bond energies.

| Bond   | H–C | C=C | H–Cl | C–C | C–Cl |
|--------|-----|-----|------|------|------|
| Bond energy in kJ/mol | 413 | X | 431 | 346 | 339 |

Calculate the bond energy X.
X = ____ kJ/mol
questions.mark_scheme:
(bonds broken = (4 × 413) + C=C + 431 =) 2083 + C=C

(bonds made = (346 + 339 + (5 × 413) =) 2750

(energy released = bonds made – bonds broken =)
56 = 2750 – [2083 + C=C]

(C=C) = 611 (kJ/mol)
questions.extra_notes: allow correct use of incorrect value(s) from step 1 and / or step 2

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
This question is about elements and compounds.
Figure 8 shows a reactivity series.
Figure 8
Most reactive
Potassium
Magnesium
Metal Y
Carbon
Iron
Hydrogen
Copper
Least reactive
Give the method and conditions used to extract metal Y from a compound of metal Y.
questions.mark_scheme:
electrolysis of molten compound (of metal Y)
OR
displacement
by heating with a more reactive metal 
or
by heating with potassium / magnesium
questions.extra_notes: allow liquid for molten

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Sodium reacts with titanium chloride (TiCl4) to produce titanium.
Complete the equation.
You should balance the equation.
Na + TiCl4 → _____ + _____
questions.mark_scheme: 4 Na + TiCl4 → 4 NaCl + Ti
questions.extra_notes:
allow multiples
allow 1 mark for NaCl and Ti with incorrect / no balancing

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
The reaction between sodium and titanium chloride is a redox reaction.
Write a half-equation to show that sodium is oxidised in this reaction.
questions.mark_scheme: Na → Na⁺ + e–
questions.extra_notes:
ignore state symbols
allow multiples
allow 1 mark for Na → Na+ + e– with incorrect balancing

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
108 g of aluminum reacts with 1.21 kg of copper chloride to produce copper.
The equation for the reaction is:
2Al + 3 CuCl2 → 3 Cu + 2AlCl3
Calculate the maximum mass of copper produced in grams (g).
You should determine the limiting reactant.
Relative atomic masses (Ar): Al = 27 Cu = 63.5
Relative formula masses (Mr): CuCl2 = 134.5 AlCl3 = 133.5
Limiting reactant is ______
Mass of copper = ____ g
questions.mark_scheme:
method 1:

(moles of Al = 108/27 =) 4

(moles CuCl2 = 1210/134.5 =) 8.996

(identifying limiting reactant)
4 moles Al gives 6 moles Cu
8.996 moles CuCl2 gives 8.996 moles Cu
therefore aluminium is the limiting reactant

(mass of Cu = 2 × 3 × 63.5)
= 6 × 63.5
= 381 (g)

method 2:

2Al + 3CuCl2 → 3Cu + 2AlCl3
(2×27) (3×134.5) (3×63.5)
54(g) 403.5(g) 190.5(g)
(so) 108 g Al (reacts with 807 g CuCl2) to produce 381 g Cu
(so) there is excess CuCl2
or
807 g CuCl2 is less than 1210 g CuCl2
therefore aluminium is limiting reactant

method 3:

134.5 g CuCl2 produces 63.5 g Cu
(mass conversion 1.21 kg CuCl2 =) 1210 (g)
1210 g CuCl2 produces (63.5/134.5 × 1210 =) 571 g Cu
54 g Al produces 190.5 g Cu
108 g Al produces (190.5/54 × 108 =) 381 (g)
(therefore) aluminium is the limiting reactant
questions.extra_notes:
allow 9
allow correct use of an incorrectly calculated value(s) for moles of Al and/or CuCl2
must follow on from MP3
allow correct use of an incorrect calculation of mass for Al/CuCl2/Cu
must follow on from MP4/MP5
allow correct use of an incorrect / no conversion of mass of CuCl2
must follow on from MP3 and MP5

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Sodium metal and sodium chloride are both able to conduct electricity.
Describe how sodium metal conducts electricity.
questions.mark_scheme: delocalised electrons carry (electrical) charge through the metal / sodium
questions.extra_notes:
allow free electrons
ignore throughout for through
ignore current / electricity
MP2 is dependent upon MP1

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text: Explain how sodium chloride can conduct electricity.
questions.mark_scheme:
(conducts electricity) when liquid / molten
or
(conducts electricity) in (aqueous) solution

(because) ions

(ions) are free to move
or
(ions) allow charge to flow
questions.extra_notes: allow (conducts electricity) when dissolved in water

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
This question is about carbon dioxide.
Carbon dioxide is soluble in water and forms an acidic solution.
Which ion makes the solution acidic?
questions.mark_scheme: hydrogen / H+

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Name an indicator that could be used to test if the solution is acidic.
Give the result of the test.
Indicator ___________________________
Result ______________________________
questions.mark_scheme:
(indicator) universal
(result) (green to) red / orange / yellow
OR
(indicator) (blue) litmus (1)
(result) (turns) red (1)
OR
(indicator) methyl orange (1)
(result) (turns) red (1)
MP2 is dependent upon MP1 being awarded
questions.extra_notes: ignore pH meter

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Figure 1 shows the mass of carbon dioxide that will dissolve in 1 dm3 of water at different temperatures.
Figure 1
How does the solubility of carbon dioxide change as the temperature of the water increases?
Tick (✓) one box.
questions.mark_scheme: the solubility decreases

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Carbon dioxide dissolves in water to form an acidic solution.
How does the pH of the solution change as the temperature of the water increases? Use Figure 1.
Tick (✓) one box.
questions.mark_scheme: pH of the solution increases

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Calcium carbonate reacts with hydrochloric acid to produce carbon dioxide.
The equation for the reaction is:
CaCO3(s) + 2 HCl(aq) → CaCl2(aq) + CO2(g) + H2O(x)
What is the state symbol (x) in the equation?
Tick (✓) one box.

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Figure 2 shows equipment a student used for an investigation.
Figure 2
The student investigated the volume of carbon dioxide produced when different masses of calcium carbonate react with hydrochloric acid.
Describe a method the student could use.
questions.mark_scheme:
Level 3: The method would lead to the production of a valid outcome. All key steps are identified and logically sequenced.
Level 2: The method would not necessarily lead to a valid outcome. Most steps are identified, but the plan is not fully logically sequenced.
Level 1: The method would not lead to a valid outcome. Some relevant steps are identified, but links are not made clear.
No relevant content

Indicative Content:
• measure a (stated) mass of calcium carbonate
• use a balance
• add calcium carbonate into a conical flask
• measure a (stated) volume of hydrochloric acid
• use a measuring cylinder
• add hydrochloric acid to the conical flask
• immediately place the stopper (and delivery tube) in the conical flask
• record the total volume of carbon dioxide gas
or
record volume of carbon dioxide collected in set time
or
time taken to collect fixed volume of gas
• collected in the gas syringe
• repeat
• repeat method with different masses of calcium carbonate

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Lithium hydroxide reacts with sulfuric acid to produce lithium sulfate.
The equation for the reaction is:
2 LiOH + H2SO4 → Li2SO4 + 2 H2O
What type of reaction is this?
questions.mark_scheme: neutralisation
questions.extra_notes: allow exothermic

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Calculate the relative formula mass (Mr) of sulfuric acid (H2SO4).
Relative atomic masses (Ar): H = 1 O = 16 S = 32
Relative formula mass (Mr) = ____

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Calculate the percentage by mass of oxygen in lithium sulfate (Li2SO4).
Relative atomic mass (Ar): O = 16
Relative formula mass (Mr): Li2SO4 = 110
Give your answer to 2 significant figures.
Percentage by mass of oxygen (2 significant figures) = ____ %
questions.extra_notes:
allow correct use of incorrectly determined mass of oxygen

allow a correctly calculated answer to 2 significant figures from an incorrect calculation which uses the values in the question

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
A solution of lithium sulfate contains 0.30 g of lithium sulfate in 25 cm3.
Calculate the concentration of lithium sulfate in g/dm3.
Concentration = ____ g/dm3
questions.mark_scheme:
(unit conversion)
(25 cm3 ÷ 1000) = 0.025 dm3
(conc =) 0.30/0.025 (g/dm3) = 12 (g/dm3)

alternative approach:
0.30/25 (g/cm3) = 0.012 (g/cm3)
(unit conversion)
(0.012 × 1000) = 12 (g/dm3)
questions.extra_notes:
allow correct use of incorrect / no unit conversion
 
allow correct conversion of an incorrect concentration calculation

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Sodium is in Group 1 of the periodic table.
Sodium reacts with oxygen to produce sodium oxide.
Balance the equation for the reaction.
Na + O2 → Na2O
questions.mark_scheme: 4 Na + O2 → 2 Na2O
questions.extra_notes: allow multiples

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Explain what happens to sodium atoms and to oxygen atoms when sodium reacts with oxygen to produce sodium oxide (Na2O).
Answer in terms of electrons.
questions.mark_scheme:
sodium atom loses one electron (and) oxygen atom gains two electrons (so) two sodium atoms to one oxygen atom
any one from:
• (to form) Na+ and O2-
• (to form) sodium ion(s) and oxide ion(s)
• (to form) ions with full outer shells / levels.

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Sodium burns in a gas jar of oxygen.
Figure 3 shows the apparatus.

1 _____________________
2 _____________________
questions.mark_scheme:
yellow flame
any one from:
• sodium melts
• white smoke / solid / powder
questions.extra_notes: allow orange flame

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Describe two differences in the observations if potassium is used instead of sodium.
1 _____________________
2 _____________________
questions.mark_scheme:
(potassium)
(burns with a) lilac flame
burns faster
questions.extra_notes:
allow (burns with a) different colour flame
allow more vigorous reaction

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Group 7 elements are known as the halogens.
All atoms of Group 7 elements contain protons, neutrons and electrons.

Br 35
   81

questions.extra_notes: allow an answer correctly calculated to 2 decimal places from an incorrect calculation which uses the values in the question

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Figure 4 shows the outer shells in one molecule of chlorine (Cl2).
Complete the dot and cross diagram to show the electrons in the outer shells.
Figure 4
questions.mark_scheme:
one shared pair in overlap
each chlorine atom with 6 non-bonded electrons (on outer shell)
questions.extra_notes:
allow any combination of circles, dots, crosses, e(–) for electrons

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
During electrolysis ions are discharged at the electrodes to produce elements.
A student investigates the electrolysis of sodium chloride.
Why does solid sodium chloride not conduct electricity?
questions.mark_scheme: ions are not free to move

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Sodium chloride solution conducts electricity.
Complete the sentence.
Sodium chloride also conducts electricity when _____________.
questions.mark_scheme: liquid or molten
questions.extra_notes:
allow (when) the solid is melted
ignore references to aqueous solutions
ignore references to dissolving

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Figure 5 shows the apparatus for the electrolysis of sodium chloride solution.
Figure 5
Suggest an element that could be used to make the inert electrodes.
questions.mark_scheme: carbon / graphite
questions.extra_notes: allow platinum / gold / silver

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Complete the half equation for the production of chlorine (Cl2) at the positive electrode.
Cl– → ___________ + ___________
questions.mark_scheme: 2 Cl– → Cl2 + 2 e–
questions.extra_notes:
ignore state symbols
allow 1 mark for Cl2 + e–

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Sodium chloride solution has a pH of 7
During the electrolysis of sodium chloride solution:
• hydrogen gas is produced at the negative electrode
• the pH of the solution increases.
Explain why.
questions.mark_scheme:
water (molecules) break down to produce (equal numbers of) H+ and OH– (ions)
(so) the hydrogen / H+ ions are attracted to the negative electrode (where) hydrogen ions are discharged / reduced
(because) hydrogen is less reactive than sodium
(so there is) a decrease in (the relative number of) hydrogen / H+ ions
or
(so there is) an increase in the relative number of hydroxide / OH– ions

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Acids react with some metals to produce soluble salts.
A student adds magnesium to hydrochloric acid until no more acid reacts and excess magnesium remains.
The equation for the reaction is:
Mg(s) + 2 HCl(aq) → MgCl2(aq) + H2(g)
Describe how solid magnesium chloride is obtained from the reaction mixture.
questions.mark_scheme:
filter (to remove excess magnesium)
(then) crystallisation (of magnesium chloride solution)
questions.extra_notes: allow (then) evaporation (of water from magnesium chloride solution)

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
The reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid is a redox reaction.
Explain what happens to the magnesium atoms in this reaction.
questions.mark_scheme: (magnesium atoms are) oxidised (because the atoms) lose (two) electrons

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
0.72 g of magnesium is added to 100 cm3 of hydrochloric acid.
The hydrochloric acid is in excess.
Calculate the concentration of the magnesium chloride (MgCl2) solution produced in g/dm3.
Relative atomic mass (Ar): Mg = 24
Relative formula mass (Mr): MgCl2 = 95
Concentration = ____ g/dm3
questions.mark_scheme:
(moles Mg =) 0.72/24 = 0.03
(moles Mg = moles MgCl2)
(so mass MgCl2 =) 0.03 × 95 = 2.85 (g)
(100.0 cm3 = 0.100 dm3
concentration = mass/volume )
= 2.85/0.100
= 28.5 (g/dm3)
questions.extra_notes:
allow a correct calculation using an incorrectly calculated value of moles of magnesium

allow a correct calculation using an incorrectly calculated value of moles of magnesium chloride

allow correct use of incorrectly calculated mass of magnesium chloride

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
This question is about structure and properties.
Which pair of substances both contain atoms in hexagonal rings?
Tick (✓) one box.
questions.mark_scheme: fullerenes and graphene

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Copper is a useful metal.

[2 marks]
1
2
questions.mark_scheme:
any two from:
• mass of copper produced increased (from 1900 to 2010)
• before 1930 the mass of copper produced increased slightly
• after 1930 the mass of copper produced increased rapidly
• the highest mass of copper was produced in 2010
• the lowest mass of copper was produced in 1900
• the increase in the mass of copper produced was not linear
questions.extra_notes:
allow a value in the range 1925 to 1930
allow a value in the range 1925 to 1930
allow the highest mass of copper produced was 15.8 (× 10⁹ kilograms) 
allow the lowest mass of copper produced was 0.2 (× 10⁹ kilograms)

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
A student investigated the thermal decomposition of copper carbonate.
Copper carbonate decomposes to form two products.
Figure 4 shows the apparatus.
Figure 4
This is the method used.
1. Add 1.2 g of copper carbonate to a test tube.
2. Heat the test tube and contents until the mass does not change.
3. Record the mass of the contents of the test tube after heating.
4. Repeat steps 1 to 3 with different masses of copper carbonate.
Table 1 shows the results.

Table 1
| Mass of copper carbonate in test tube before heating in grams | Mass of the contents of test tube after heating in grams |
|-------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| 1.2                                                         | 0.8                                                    |
| 2.4                                                         | 1.7                                                    |
| 3.6                                                         | 2.2                                                    |
| 4.8                                                         | 3.1                                                    |
| 6.0                                                         | 3.99                                                   |

Plot the data from Table 1 on Figure 5.
Draw a line of best fit.
Figure 5
questions.mark_scheme:
all points correctly plotted
line of best fit
questions.extra_notes:
allow a tolerance of ± ½ a small square 
allow 1 mark for 3 or 4 points correctly plotted

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text: Why does the mass of the contents of the test tube decrease in mass when copper carbonate is thermally decomposed?
questions.mark_scheme: a gas escapes (from the test tube)
questions.extra_notes:
allow carbon dioxide escapes (from the test tube)
ignore copper carbonate thermally decomposes

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Table 2 shows diagrams which represent the structures of two substances.
Table 2
Compare the structure and bonding of sodium chloride and oxygen.
questions.mark_scheme:
Level 2: Scientifically relevant features are identified; the way(s) in which they are similar/different is made clear and (where appropriate) the magnitude of the similarity/difference is noted. 4‒6
Level 1: Relevant features are identified and differences noted. 1‒3
No relevant content 0
Indicative content:
Similarities
• both have strong bonds
• particles have full outer shells
Differences
• sodium chloride is a compound
• sodium chloride is made from two elements
• oxygen is an element
• sodium chloride is a giant structure of ions
• oxygen is a small molecule
• sodium chloride has ionic bonds
• sodium chloride consists of oppositely charged ions
• electrons are transferred from sodium atoms to chlorine atoms
• oxygen is covalent
• oxygen (molecules) are made up of oxygen atoms
• joined by double covalent bond
• electrons are shared between oxygen atoms
• with weak forces between oxygen molecules
For Level 2, there must be a comparison of either the structure or bonding of both sodium chloride and oxygen, and a description of magnitude.
questions.extra_notes: For Level 2, there must be a comparison of either the structure or bonding of both sodium chloride and oxygen, and a description of magnitude.

questions.mark_scheme:
one shared pair of electrons in each overlap

questions.extra_notes:

ignore any inner shell electrons on nitrogen
do not accept non-bonding electrons on hydrogen
an answer of
scores 2 marks

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
The equation for the reaction between nitrogen and hydrogen to produce ammonia is:
N2 + 3H2 → 2NH3
Calculate the mass of hydrogen that is needed to produce 25 g of ammonia.
Relative atomic masses (Ar): H = 1 N = 14
Mass of hydrogen = ____ g
questions.mark_scheme:
(Mr NH₃ = 14 + (3 × 1) =) 17
(moles NH₃ = 25/17) = 1.47 (mols)
(moles H₂ = 1.47 × 3/2 )
= 2.205 (mols)
(mass H₂ = 2.205 × 2)
= 4.41 (g)
alternative approach
(2 × Mr NH₃ = 17 × 2 =) 34
6 g H₂ → 34 g NH₃
6/34 × 25 (g) H₂ → 25 g NH₃
4.41 (g H₂ → 25 g NH₃)
questions.extra_notes:
allow correct use of incorrectly determined Mr of NH3
allow correct use of an incorrectly calculated value of moles of NH3
allow 4.4117647 (g) correctly rounded to at least 2 significant figures
allow correct use of an incorrectly calculated value of moles of H2
allow correct use of an incorrectly calculated value of 2 × Mr NH3

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
Figure 7 shows the displayed formulae equation for the reaction of nitrogen with hydrogen.
Figure 7
In the reaction the energy released forming new bonds is 93 kJ/mol greater than the energy needed to break existing bonds.
Table 3 shows bond energies.

Table 3
| Bond        | H — H | N — H |
|-------------|-------|-------|
| Bond energy in kJ/mol | 945 | X | 391 |

Calculate the bond energy X for the H — H bond. 
Use Figure 7 and Table 3. 
X = ____ kJ/mol
questions.mark_scheme:
(bonds broken =)
945 + 3X
(bonds made = 6 × 391 =) 2346
(energy released = bonds made – bonds broken =)
93 = 2346 – [945 + 3X]
(3X =) 1308 (kJ/mol)
(X =) 436 (kJ/mol)
questions.extra_notes:
allow correct use of incorrectly determined value(s) of bonds broken and/or bonds formed
allow correct use of an incorrectly determined value for 3X

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H

• label the energy level of the reactants and the product
• label the overall energy change.
questions.mark_scheme:
correct shape for exothermic reaction with the product line below the level of the reactants line
labelled horizontal lines for reactants and product
labelled overall energy change
labelled horizontal lines for nitrogen and hydrogen and ammonia
questions.extra_notes:
an answer of
scores 3 marks

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H

questions.exam: AQA GCSE Combined Science: Chemistry Paper 1H
questions.question_text:
